Drought-tolerant transgenic wheat HB4®: a hope for the future
================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Pushpendra K.Gupta 1 2 3

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tibtech.2023.12.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tibtech.2023.12.007 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0167779923003463&orderBeanReset=true)

Abstract
--------

Drought-tolerant transgenic [genetically modified (GM)] HB4® wheat carrying the drought-responsive [sunflower](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/helianthus-annuus "Learn more about sunflower from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") gene _Hahb4_ was first developed in Argentina in 2019 and has already been approved for marketing and consumption as food/feed in at least ten countries. It has also been approved in Argentina and Brazil for commercial cultivation.

Section snippets
----------------

Transgenic (GM) crops
---------------------

Production of transgenic crops, also called GM crops, is one of several recent approaches including marker-assisted selection (MAS) and gene editing that have now become available to supplement conventional plant breeding. During the past 30 years, a number of transgenic crops have been approved for cultivation and are being grown in around 30 countries, despite the difficult biosafety/regulatory issues that are associated with this technology. The global area occupied by GM crops started

History of HB4® wheat
---------------------

The history of HB4® GM wheat can be traced back to 2004, when research groups in Argentina – a team led by Raquel Chan, Director of Instituto de Agrobiotecnología del Litoral de la Universidad Nacional del Litoral (Institute of Agrobiotechnology of the National University of Littoral, Argentina), along with the members of CONICET (National Scientific and Technological Research Council, Argentina) – patented a genetic construct/event described as IND-ØØ412-7. This genetic construct was used to

Safety assessment and approval of HB4® wheat
--------------------------------------------

During the past few years, several countries reported no public health or safety concerns associated with HB4® wheat, and that products derived from this GM wheat were as safe for human consumption as the food derived from conventional non-GM wheat cultivars. The safety assessment was based on a number of key elements, including characterization of the transferred gene (novel nucleic acids) and protein in the whole food (if any), detailed compositional analyses of the products, evaluation of

Conclusions and future perspectives
-----------------------------------

Drought is one of the major limitations in achieving the required increase in future food production. However, a 10–20% increase in wheat/soybean production under water stress has been witnessed in Argentina due to HB4 technology. Therefore, the development and acceptance of HB4® wheat and soybean should be looked upon with some hope and expectation. Hopefully, in these two crops, HB4 technology should also enter conventional breeding programs, with the hope that drought tolerance will be

Cited by (5)

*   ### [Insights into wheat science: A bibliometric review using unsupervised machine learning techniques](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0733521024001188)

2024, Journal of Cereal Science Citation Excerpt : 
This research focus aligns with the overarching goal of wheat research, which centres on enhancing crop productivity to meet the demands of a growing global population (Araus et al., 2019). By improving agronomic traits such as grain yield and plant growth through genetic modification, researchers aim to achieve higher harvest values relative to the allocated agricultural area, and concerns about climate change (Gupta, 2024). Increasing wheat yield is crucial for ensuring food security and economic stability, particularly in regions where wheat serves as a staple food crop.   Show abstract Wheat (_Triticum_ spp.) has been one of the most important cereal crops, serving as a source of protein and energy in the human diet. It remains a vital component of global food security, with extensive scientific literature dedicated to its study, although the large volume of literature often hinders global analysis. In this study, different unsupervised machine learning techniques, such as K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) and Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), text mining analyses, including word embeddings and statistical word analysis, and graph analysis methodologies, were applied to gain a deeper understanding of the wheat literature. The proposed bibliometric analysis was conducted and integrated with the Journal Citation Reports (JCR) to identify major wheat research trends in the PubMed literature. This analysis examined the evolution of these trends over time, evaluated the geographical distribution, impact, and research domains, and assessed author collaboration networks and the evolving relevance of different countries. Research on disease resistance, genetic modification, and dietary impact demonstrates a consistent increase in output, while interest in topics related to overcoming salt stress and enhancing animal feed appears to be diminishing. Interestingly, research on wheat germ agglutinin saw a surge in interest during the late 2000s, stabilizing thereafter. These trends underscore the dynamic nature of wheat research, driven by evolving priorities and technological advancements, particularly in genetics and omics tools. Moreover, the increasing significance of China in wheat research, including its size, impact, and networking, alongside longstanding leaders such as the United States, signals a shifting landscape in global wheat research.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Investing in scientific research for GM crops enhances drought tolerance, improving yields and sustainability, as demonstrated in studies on transgenic wheat and other crops.
Increasing public knowledge about GM crops' benefits, like drought resistance and food security, fosters acceptance and informed decision-making in agricultural practices.

enriched_points.point_title: Encourage Policy Changes for GM Crop Approval
enriched_points.expanded_content: Advocating for streamlined regulatory frameworks accelerates the approval of GM crops, such as drought-tolerant varieties, ensuring timely access to innovative agricultural solutions.
Integrating GM crops within sustainable practices, including crop rotation and conservation tillage, enhances resilience against climate change and ensures long-term productivity.
Utilizing drought-tolerant GM crops plays a crucial role in addressing food scarcity amidst climate challenges, ensuring stable food supplies for growing populations worldwide.
